title: Beowulf: An Anglo-Saxon Epic Poem
author: Unknown Author
summary: Beowulf tells how Hrothgar, king of the Danes, builds the great mead-hall Heorot, which is terrorized for years by the monster Grendel. Beowulf, a Geatish hero and nephew of King Higelac, sails with followers to Dane-land, confronts Grendel, and mortally wounds him in hand-to-hand combat. Grendel’s mother later attacks; Beowulf dives to her watery lair and slays her, bringing trophies home. He returns to Geatland, becomes king, and after fifty years faces a fire‑breathing dragon. Although he and the dragon die, Beowulf’s valor, generosity, and fame endure in a solemn national funeral. It emphasizes joy, loyalty, gratitude, gift-giving, and mourning.

characters.name: Beowulf
characters.age: young adult (~30s) at first; aged (~70s) at death
characters.lifespan: Arrives to aid Hrothgar, kills Grendel and Grendel's mother, returns home, becomes king of the Geats, rules fifty years, dies fighting the dragon.

characters.name: Hrothgar
characters.lifespan: Ruler of the Danes who builds Heorot; receives Beowulf's aid and gifts; survives the immediate tale and mourns departures.

characters.name: Grendel
characters.lifespan: Terrorizes Heorot for years; attacked by Beowulf and mortally wounded, retreats to his lair and dies.

characters.name: Grendel's Mother
characters.lifespan: Avenges her son by attacking Heorot, is pursued by Beowulf into her underwater den and slain by him.

characters.name: Scyld
characters.lifespan: Introduced in 'The Life and Death of Scyld' as an early Danish king whose reign and successors are recounted.

characters.name: Scyldings (the Danes)
characters.lifespan: People of Hrothgar who suffer Grendel's depredations and celebrate Beowulf's victories.

characters.name: Higelac
characters.lifespan: King of the Geats who receives Beowulf on his return; Beowulf later succeeds in rulership among the Geats.

characters.name: Unferth
characters.personality: skeptical, taunting, envious but present in court
characters.lifespan: Taunts Beowulf at Heorot and is later silenced by him; remains a court figure.

characters.name: Wiglaf
characters.lifespan: Stands with Beowulf in the final fight against the dragon, rebukes fleeing warriors, survives after Beowulf's death.

characters.name: Finn
characters.age: adult leader in the scop's song
characters.lifespan: Character in the Scop's song and Finn episode recited during the banquet; central to that digression.

characters.name: Hnæf
characters.lifespan: Figure in the Finn episode and Scop's song recited at the feast.

characters.name: Scop (the bard/poet)
characters.lifespan: Sings the Finn episode and other lays during the banquet at Heorot.

characters.name: Hrothgar's favorite counsellor
characters.lifespan: Seized and killed by Grendel's mother in revenge, prompting Beowulf's quest to the lair.

characters.name: The Two Queens
characters.personality: dignified, ceremonial, figures of courtly presence
characters.lifespan: Appear in the narrative as royal women associated with courts (unnamed in provided text).

characters.name: The Dragon
characters.personality: vengeful, fire‑spewing, guardian of a hoard
characters.lifespan: Harasses Geatland late in Beowulf's reign; fights Beowulf, both die in the encounter.

characters.name: Thanes/Retainers
characters.lifespan: Serve their lords in hall and battle; many are killed by Grendel or desert in dragon fight.

characters.name: Fourteen Companions (Beowulf's chosen men)
characters.lifespan: Accompany Beowulf to Dane-land; participate in the adventure at Heorot.

characters.name: The Messenger of Death
characters.lifespan: Provides retrospection and commentary later in the poem (as titled in contents).

characters.name: The Dead Foes (group)
characters.lifespan: Referenced among trophies and in the aftermath of battles.

locations.name: Geatland (region of the Geats, southern Sweden / Götaland)
North Sea (the sea traversed by Beowulf to Dane-land)

unusual_words.definition: worthy of respect due to age or dignity
unusual_words.context: Used in preface describing the 'venerable epic'.

unusual_words.definition: long narrative poem about heroic deeds
unusual_words.context: Describes the poem 'Beowulf' as an epic.

unusual_words.definition: rhythmic flow of sounds in language
unusual_words.context: Translator discusses the cadences used to render Anglo-Saxon verse.

unusual_words.definition: an initial unstressed syllable(s) before the first strong beat
unusual_words.context: Mentioned as a metrical device allowed in the translation.

unusual_words.definition: repetition of initial consonant sounds in nearby words
unusual_words.context: Discussed as a key feature of Anglo-Saxon poetry used in translation.

unusual_words.definition: rhyme at the ends of lines
unusual_words.context: Translator notes occasional use of end-rhyme.

unusual_words.definition: rhyme within a line of verse
unusual_words.context: Translator notes sporadic internal rhyme in the original.

unusual_words.definition: term used for the element that supplies alliteration in half-verses
unusual_words.context: Gummere's 'rime-giver' is discussed regarding alliteration rules.

unusual_words.definition: a word or style from an earlier period retained for effect
unusual_words.context: Translator sought an archaic flavor in diction.

unusual_words.definition: old-fashioned or ancient in style
unusual_words.context: Used to describe certain words chosen for the translation.

unusual_words.definition: non-specialist or ordinary reader
unusual_words.context: Translator mentions laymen in reference to earlier prose translations.

unusual_words.definition: communal hall where warriors drank mead and feasted
unusual_words.context: Hrothgar's great mead-hall Heorot is central to the story.

unusual_words.definition: magnificent residence of a sovereign
unusual_words.context: Heorot is described as a palace in which Hrothgar feasts his liegemen.

unusual_words.definition: vassals or loyal retainers of a lord
unusual_words.context: Hrothgar hopes to feast his liegemen in Heorot.

unusual_words.definition: gifts bestowed by a lord upon followers
unusual_words.context: Hrothgar's hopes to give presents to his retainers.

unusual_words.definition: name of the monster antagonist
unusual_words.context: Central monster who attacks Heorot.

unusual_words.definition: a dwelling in a marsh or bog
unusual_words.context: Grendel is said to live in a fen-dwelling.

unusual_words.definition: poetic epithet for a creature that strides; 'march' walker
unusual_words.context: Used in the story to characterize Grendel's gait.

unusual_words.definition: a noble or warrior in Anglo-Saxon society
unusual_words.context: Thanes are carried off by Grendel.

unusual_words.definition: subordinate followers bound to a lord
unusual_words.context: Used to describe those serving Hrothgar.

unusual_words.definition: Denmark; land of the Danes
unusual_words.context: Beowulf sails to Dane-land to help Hrothgar.

unusual_words.definition: name of the poem's hero
unusual_words.context: Hero of the epic who comes to aid Hrothgar.

unusual_words.definition: the people of Geatland (Beowulf's people)
unusual_words.context: Beowulf is a Geat, nephew of King Higelac.

unusual_words.definition: king of the Geats, Beowulf's uncle
unusual_words.context: Receives Beowulf on his return.

unusual_words.definition: a sovereign or lord to whom others owe allegiance
unusual_words.context: Beowulf is offered as next liegelord among Hrothgar's vassals.

unusual_words.definition: responsibility for protecting or guarding
unusual_words.context: Hrothgar gives wardship of his palace to Beowulf for the night.

unusual_words.definition: name of Hrothgar's mead-hall
unusual_words.context: Central meeting place attacked by Grendel.

unusual_words.definition: a grasp with the hand; decisive grip in combat
unusual_words.context: Beowulf and Grendel fight trusting to strength and hand-grip.

unusual_words.definition: part of the body; used narratively
unusual_words.context: Beowulf tears Grendel's shoulder from its socket.

unusual_words.definition: a loud mournful cry
unusual_words.context: Grendel retreats howling with pain.

unusual_words.definition: early morning
unusual_words.context: At early dawn warriors flock to Heorot after the battle.

unusual_words.definition: joyousness or merriment
unusual_words.context: The hall's glee runs high after Grendel's defeat.

unusual_words.definition: advisors to the king
unusual_words.context: One of Hrothgar's favorite counsellors is carried off by Grendel's mother.

unusual_words.definition: lair or lair-like dwelling of a wild creature
unusual_words.context: Grendel and his mother have dens beneath the water.

unusual_words.definition: the floor of the sea
unusual_words.context: Beowulf meets Grendel's mother near the sea-bottom.

unusual_words.definition: objects taken as spoils of victory
unusual_words.context: Beowulf brings Grendel's head and other trophies to the hall.

unusual_words.definition: stored treasure or wealth
unusual_words.context: A hoard is connected with the dragon episode.

unusual_words.definition: mythical fire-breathing beast
unusual_words.context: A dragon later harasses Beowulf's land and fights him.

unusual_words.definition: a heap of combustible material for burning a corpse
unusual_words.context: A great funeral-pyre is built to burn Beowulf's body.

unusual_words.definition: a burial mound raised as a memorial
unusual_words.context: A barrow is built to mark Beowulf's burial site.

unusual_words.definition: Anglo-Saxon bard or poet
unusual_words.context: The Scop sings the Finn song at the banquet.

unusual_words.definition: figure whose story is told by the Scop
unusual_words.context: The Finn episode is recited during the banquet.

unusual_words.definition: character in the Finn episode
unusual_words.context: Named in the Scop's Song included in the banquet.

unusual_words.definition: a Danish courtier who taunts Beowulf
unusual_words.context: Unferth challenges Beowulf's reputation and is later silenced.

unusual_words.definition: Beowulf's loyal kinsman and companion
unusual_words.context: Wiglaf aids Beowulf in the final battle against the dragon.

unusual_words.definition: early Danish king introduced in the poem
unusual_words.context: The Life and Death of Scyld is the opening section.

unusual_words.definition: the dynasty or people of Scyld (Danes)
unusual_words.context: Used as a term for Hrothgar's people.

unusual_words.definition: king of the Danes, builder of Heorot
unusual_words.context: Hrothgar rules the Danes and is aided by Beowulf.

unusual_words.definition: one who follows another in office
unusual_words.context: Scyld's successors are discussed early in the poem.

unusual_words.definition: summarized or presented as an epitome
unusual_words.context: The translator calls the poem an epitomized history of Teutonic life.

unusual_words.definition: relating to Germanic peoples
unusual_words.context: The poem is described as history of the Teutonic races.

unusual_words.definition: relating to the period before King Alfred
unusual_words.context: The translator situates the poem in pre-Alfredian eras.

unusual_words.definition: relating to a caesura, a metrical pause in verse
unusual_words.context: No two accented syllables are brought together except sometimes after a cæsural pause.

unusual_words.definition: stressed part in metrical terms (here: stress of a foot)
unusual_words.context: The translator allows as much thesis and anacrusis as fits cadence.

unusual_words.definition: repetition of similar grammatical structures
unusual_words.context: Parallelisms of the original have been preserved in the translation.

unusual_words.definition: critic's corrections or proposed textual changes
unusual_words.context: Translator occasionally adds his own conjectural emendations.

unusual_words.definition: German term for alliterative verse ('stave-rhymed')
unusual_words.context: Used in bibliography to describe Ettmuller's translation.

unusual_words.definition: French for 'epic'
unusual_words.context: Botkine's French translation titled 'Beowulf. Epopée Anglo-Saxonne'.

unusual_words.definition: reference to the Heyne-Socin text edition
unusual_words.context: The translation follows the Heyne-Socin text and glossary.

unusual_words.definition: name of a scholar (Sievers) associated with metrical types
unusual_words.context: Sievers's metrical types are discussed regarding translation choices.

unusual_words.definition: accented beats in a verse line
unusual_words.context: Anglo-Saxon verse's four stresses are retained in the translation.

unusual_words.definition: one who wields (used poetically as an epithet)
unusual_words.context: Example 'Wielder of Glory' appears in illustrating parallelism.

unusual_words.definition: working together
unusual_words.context: Translator asks for the sympathy and co-operation of readers.

unusual_words.definition: to express grief or sorrow
unusual_words.context: Hrothgar weeps and laments at Beowulf's departure.

unusual_words.definition: valuable gifts or riches
unusual_words.context: Hrothgar pours treasures into Beowulf's lap as rewards.

unusual_words.definition: a speculative emendation or hypothesis
unusual_words.context: Translator sometimes adds his own conjecture to editorial notes.

unusual_words.definition: renown or praise (used as epithet)
unusual_words.context: Used in parallel epithets in the translation examples.

unusual_words.definition: wrath or ire
unusual_words.context: Grendel is said to bear 'God's anger' in his attacks.

unusual_words.definition: without pity or compassion
unusual_words.context: Thanes are ruthlessly carried off and devoured by Grendel.

unusual_words.definition: harasses or oppresses repeatedly
unusual_words.context: Grendel persecutes Hrothgar and his vassals for twelve years.

unusual_words.definition: deadly or fierce (poetic adjective)
unusual_words.context: Beowulf resolves to crush the fell monster.

unusual_words.definition: one who saves or rescues
unusual_words.context: Hrothgar lavishes gifts upon his deliverer, Beowulf.

unusual_words.definition: folds of cloth or metaphorical lap where gifts are placed
unusual_words.context: Treasures are poured into Beowulf's lap.

unusual_words.definition: exceptional bravery or skill in battle
unusual_words.context: Beowulf's prowess is lauded in the closing tribute.

unusual_words.definition: quality of being generous
unusual_words.context: Beowulf's generosity is praised after his death.

unusual_words.definition: metrical pattern or rhythm
unusual_words.context: Translator claims to reproduce Anglo-Saxon measure in modern form.

unusual_words.definition: ardent or enthusiastic (figurative)
unusual_words.context: The poem closes with a glowing tribute to Beowulf.

unusual_words.definition: followers or household warriors
unusual_words.context: Hrothgar feasts his retainers in Heorot.

unusual_words.definition: realm or domain of a king
unusual_words.context: Beowulf becomes king of the Geats and rules his kingdom.

unusual_words.definition: plagued or troubled persistently
unusual_words.context: Beowulf's neighborhood is woefully harried by the dragon.

unusual_words.definition: breathing or emitting fire
unusual_words.context: Describes the dragon as fire-spewing.

unusual_words.definition: the act of departing or farewell
unusual_words.context: There is sorrow at parting when Beowulf leaves Dane-land.

unusual_words.definition: a pyre for burning a corpse
unusual_words.context: A great funeral-pyre is built to burn Beowulf's body.

unusual_words.definition: to gather in a crowd
unusual_words.context: Warriors flock to Heorot at the dawn after Grendel's defeat.

unusual_words.definition: a lavish feast
unusual_words.context: Many sections of the poem describe banquets and feasting.

unusual_words.definition: reflections on past events
unusual_words.context: Sections titled 'Reminiscences' recount past deeds.

unusual_words.definition: formal expression of praise or homage
unusual_words.context: The poem ends with a tribute to Beowulf's virtues.

unusual_words.definition: harsh or resentful in tone
unusual_words.context: Wiglaf's bitter taunts are mentioned in contents.

unusual_words.definition: insults or jeers intended to provoke
unusual_words.context: Wiglaf's bitter taunts are part of the aftermath.

unusual_words.definition: to pillage or take by force
unusual_words.context: Wiglaf plunders the dragon's den after the fight.

unusual_words.definition: one who delivers news or information
unusual_words.context: Sections titled 'The Messenger of Death' and 'The Messenger's Retrospect' appear.

unusual_words.definition: a review or contemplation of past events
unusual_words.context: The messenger offers a retrospect of events.

unusual_words.definition: the act of setting a body afire for funeral rites
unusual_words.context: The burning of Beowulf is a named section.

unusual_words.definition: supplementary material added at the end
unusual_words.context: Addenda appears as a section in the contents.

unusual_words.definition: introductory remarks by the translator
unusual_words.context: The book includes a substantial Preface explaining translation choices.

unusual_words.definition: a list of sources and translations
unusual_words.context: A bibliography of translations is provided.

unusual_words.definition: a list explaining proper names and terms
unusual_words.context: A Glossary of Proper Names is included in the book.

unusual_words.definition: shortened forms of words or names
unusual_words.context: An 'ABBREVIATIONS USED IN THE NOTES' section lists scholarly abbreviations.

unusual_words.definition: the classical language of ancient Rome
unusual_words.context: Some earlier translations and notes reference Latin translations.

unusual_words.definition: unrhymed iambic pentameter used in English verse
unusual_words.context: Conybeare's and other translations included passages in blank-verse.
